file.content:
# Week 6.5 — Agent sandboxing + multi-provider LLM design

**Status:** design, pre-implementation-plan.
**Predecessors:** Weeks 1–2, 3, 4–6.
**Successors:** Week 7–9 (k8s deployment shape) consumes this contract.

---

## Goal

Make the agent loop pluggable and move it out of the host process, so that:

1. Users can pick their LLM provider (Anthropic, OpenRouter, future OpenAI/Gemini) for cost savings.
2. Users can pick their agent framework (our own loop, `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent` / pi-session, Anthropic's `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk`) for capability tradeoffs.
3. Agent execution runs in a sandbox pod in production and a subprocess-sandbox in local dev, with **one code path** — the same IPC protocol in every mode.
4. Either host or sandbox can terminate unexpectedly without losing the session (any host replica can serve any sandbox; durable inbox; clean reconnect).

## Motivation

Current Week 4-6 shipped a host-local chat loop (`packages/core/src/chat-loop.ts`). That's the wrong topology for production — tools execute in the host process, which violates the capability-minimization invariant (I5). It also doesn't compose with pi-session or claude-agent-sdk: both are SDK-owned-loop frameworks that run as their own process with tools resolved via callbacks, not providers pluggable into our loop.

v1 has already proven the target shape (host↔sandbox HTTP IPC, pull-based inbox, per-session workspace commits via git). We're porting the pattern and fixing v1's two known weaknesses: in-memory inbox bound to a single host replica (no failover) and no graceful cancellation path.

## Scope: four sequenced slices

Each slice is independently reviewable with a clean "nothing observable changes except the intended thing" acceptance. Smaller than 6.5a-as-one-blob (which would be bigger than Week 4–6 was) and targets risk where it actually lives — the topology shift dominates difficulty; providers and alternative runners are additive.

| Slice | Ships | Acceptance |
|---|---|---|
| **6.5a — topology shift** | IPC protocol, IPC server, `sandbox:open-session`, `@ax/session-inmemory`, `@ax/chat-orchestrator`, `@ax/agent-runner-core`, `@ax/agent-native-runner`, tool refactor (host→sandbox). Still `@ax/llm-anthropic`; no streaming. | Existing Week 4–6 chat works end-to-end with the agent loop running in a subprocess sandbox; all existing subscribers fire correctly across the IPC boundary; no observable behavior change vs. Week 4–6. |
| **6.5b — providers + streaming** | `@ax/llm-openrouter` + streaming extensions to `llm.call` + `chat:stream-chunk` subscriber hook + native runner emits stream chunks + CLI renders incrementally. | Toggle provider via config; CLI renders responses incrementally. |
| **6.5c — pi-session runner** | `@ax/agent-pi-session-runner` + runner-selection config. | Swap to pi-session runner via config; audit/subscribers fire identically; works with every `@ax/llm-*` plugin (no runner-side changes per provider). |
| **6.5d — claude-sdk runner** | `@ax/agent-claude-sdk-runner` + `@ax/llm-proxy-anthropic-format` (host-side Anthropic-format proxy translating `POST /v1/messages` → `llm.call` IPC). | Swap to claude-sdk runner via config; claude's built-in tools + our IPC pre/post work together; LLM routes through proxy. |

This doc specifies all four. The implementation-plan session writes the plan for 6.5a first.

---

## Core design decisions

### D1. Agent loop runs in the sandbox, not the host

The chat loop that lives in `packages/core/src/chat-loop.ts` today moves into a sandbox-side runner binary. The host orchestrates but does not execute the loop.

Why:
- Tool execution inherits the sandbox's isolation boundary (I5).
- pi-session and claude-agent-sdk both own their internal loops. A pluggable loop on the host can't embed them cleanly.
- v1 runs this way; the pattern is validated.

Cost: we lose in-process speed (every LLM call is an IPC round-trip). Acceptable — LLM latency dominates; IPC overhead is sub-millisecond over a unix socket.

### D2. Sandbox is always the HTTP client; host never pushes

All IPC traffic is sandbox-initiated. The host runs one HTTP listener (unix socket locally, cluster HTTP in k8s); the sandbox has no inbound port and serves nothing.

Why:
- Single auth boundary (every inbound request to host is token-verified; sandbox grants no new capabilities).
- Sandbox is the untrusted side; fewer listening sockets = smaller attack surface.
- Matches v1's pattern.

### D3. Host→sandbox delivery is pull-based via durable inbox

When the host needs to deliver something to a running sandbox (follow-up user message, cancellation), it writes to a durable inbox keyed by `session_id`. The sandbox long-polls.

| Preset | Inbox backing |
|---|---|
| Local / single-host | In-memory queue in `@ax/session-inmemory` |
| k8s (Week 7–9) | Postgres table in `@ax/session-postgres`, with `LISTEN/NOTIFY` wakeup on long-polls |

Consequences:
- Any host replica can serve any sandbox's poll. No sticky sessions, no replica affinity.
- Host replica can crash mid-poll — sandbox's next poll hits a different replica, continues.
- Sandbox pod eviction detected by absence of polls beyond a heartbeat threshold; session marked terminated.
- No NATS, no JetStream, no second message broker. Inbox is a table with long-poll.

Cancellation is an inbox entry of type `cancel`. Runner handles it gracefully and exits.

### D4. `tool:post-call` is observation-only; `llm:pre-call` is the transformation point

`claude-agent-sdk`'s `PostToolUse` hook is observation-only in practice — the `updatedMCPToolOutput` field is declared in the type but not read by `cli.js`. To keep subscriber behavior uniform across all three runners, we adopt observation-only for `tool:post-call` everywhere.

Subscribers that need to **modify** content the model will see (canary redaction, memory recall injection, prompt restructuring) shift to `llm:pre-call`, which fires host-side right before every LLM request.

Subscribers that just **observe** tool calls (audit, telemetry, memory extraction) stay on `tool:post-call`.

Why this is actually better than tool-level redaction:
- `llm:pre-call` transformation catches secrets from every source, not just fresh tool output.
- Audit logs retain the raw, un-redacted tool output for forensics.
- Uniform across runners, tools, and execution locations (sandbox-local or host-mediated).

### D5. Three runners, one IPC contract

| Runner | Slice | Tool path | LLM path |
|---|---|---|---|
| `@ax/agent-native-runner` | 6.5a | Direct handlers with IPC pre/post | `llm.call` IPC directly |
| `@ax/agent-pi-session-runner` | 6.5c | pi-session `customTools` callbacks with IPC pre/post | `registerApiProvider` (single `ax-ipc` registration) routes through `llm.call` IPC |
| `@ax/agent-claude-sdk-runner` | 6.5d | Hybrid: claude-sdk built-ins + in-process MCP handlers, both wired via `canUseTool` + `hooks.PreToolUse`/`PostToolUse` | `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` points at host-side Anthropic-format proxy → `llm.call` IPC |

All three fire the same host-side subscriber hooks via the same IPC events. Host observers (audit, canary, memory) don't know which runner is active.

---

## The sandbox ladder

Three sandbox implementations, identical `sandbox:open-session` service hook contract.

| Plugin | Isolation | When | 6.5a? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `@ax/sandbox-subprocess` | None — subprocess of host, shared FS & network | CI, fastest dev iteration, integration tests | ✅ extended |
| `@ax/sandbox-docker` | Docker container / Apple container — real FS + network isolation | Realistic local dev | Stretch |
| `@ax/sandbox-k8s` | k8s pod — full isolation, RBAC, NetworkPolicy | Production | Week 7–9 |

All three spawn the runner binary, inject env (`AX_IPC_SOCKET` or `AX_IPC_URL`, `AX_SESSION_ID`, `AX_AUTH_TOKEN`), return a handle with `kill()`. The runner binary is identical in every mode. `@ax/sandbox-subprocess`'s total lack of isolation is fine for dev and CI; production never loads it.

**Cleanup note:** Week 4–6's existing `sandbox:spawn` one-shot primitive (used by `@ax/tool-bash`) loses its host-side caller — bash moves into the sandbox. Delete in 6.5a. Revive only when a real host-side one-shot-subprocess consumer arrives (YAGNI).

---

## IPC protocol

### Transport

HTTP+JSON. Transport varies by sandbox plugin:

| Sandbox plugin | Transport |
|---|---|
| `@ax/sandbox-subprocess` | Unix socket at `${AX_IPC_SOCKET}` |
| `@ax/sandbox-docker` | Unix socket bind-mounted into container |
| `@ax/sandbox-k8s` | Cluster HTTP — host exposes a Service reachable from pods |

Same wire format, same actions. The runner reads `AX_IPC_SOCKET` (socket path) or `AX_IPC_URL` (http URL), otherwise indistinguishable.

### Auth

Per-session bearer token issued at `sandbox:open-session` time. Stored server-side in the session store (postgres in k8s, in-memory locally). Any host replica resolves the token against the store.

Token lifetime = sandbox session lifetime. No per-turn rotation (v1's per-turn rotation was a workaround for its in-memory-replica problem, which we fix structurally).

Revocation: mark session terminated in the store; subsequent IPC calls with that token return 401.

### Sandbox → host actions (sync RPC)

| Action | Payload | Response | Host-side effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| `llm.call` | `{ messages, tools?, model?, maxTokens?, temperature? }` | `{ assistantMessage, toolCalls[], stopReason, usage? }` | Fires `llm:pre-call` (transform), calls `llm:call` service hook, fires `llm:post-call` (observe) |
| `tool.pre-call` | `{ call: { id, name, input } }` | `{ verdict: 'allow' \| 'reject', modifiedCall?, reason? }` | Fires `tool:pre-call` subscriber chain |
| `tool.execute-host` | `{ call: { id, name, input } }` | `{ output }` | Dispatches `tool:execute:${name}` service hook (used for `executesIn: 'host'` tools) |
| `tool.list` | `{}` | `{ tools: ToolDescriptor[] }` | Returns the aggregated catalog |
| `workspace.commit-notify` | `{ parentVersion?, commitRef, message }` | `{ accepted: true, version, delta } \| { accepted: false, reason }` | Host's `@ax/workspace-git` fetches delta, fires `workspace:pre-apply` (veto-capable) → on accept: promotes + fires `workspace:applied` |
| `session.next-message` | `?cursor=N` query param; long-poll up to 30s | `{ type: 'user-message' \| 'cancel' \| 'timeout', payload?, cursor }` | Reads inbox, long-poll wakeup on NOTIFY |

### Sandbox → host events (fire-and-forget)

| Action | Payload | Host-side effect |
|---|---|---|
| `event.stream-chunk` | `{ reqId, text, kind: 'text' \| 'thinking' }` | Fires `chat:stream-chunk` subscriber (chat-ui SSE, CLI tty) |
| `event.tool-post-call` | `{ call, output, durationMs }` | Fires `tool:post-call` subscriber chain (observation only, per D4) |
| `event.turn-end` | `{ reqId, reason: 'user-message-wait' \| 'error' \| 'complete', usage? }` | Fires `chat:turn-end` subscriber |
| `event.chat-end` | `{ outcome: ChatOutcome }` | Fires `chat:end` subscriber |

### Host → sandbox actions

**None.** Host never pushes. All host→sandbox communication is via `session.next-message` responses.

### Error model

Every RPC response that isn't a success carries `{ error: { code, message } }`. Codes are enum-ish:
- `SESSION_INVALID` — token rejected, sandbox should exit
- `HOST_UNAVAILABLE` — retryable (replica died; sandbox reconnects)
- `VALIDATION` — payload schema error
- `HOOK_REJECTED` — subscriber chain rejected (e.g., `tool:pre-call` verdict reject)
- `INTERNAL` — unexpected host-side error

Connection-level errors (socket close, HTTP error): sandbox retries with exponential backoff, capped at ~30s. Host session persists in the store; the next successful request resumes.

### Schema location

Every action schema lives in `@ax/ipc-protocol` — one pnpm package, pure types + Zod, no runtime. Both host-side plugins and sandbox-side runner import it. Single source of truth for the wire.

---

## Host-side plugin decomposition

Per slice. Exactly-one-registrar per service hook is enforced at boot, so presets load one `llm:*` plugin at a time.

### 6.5a

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `@ax/ipc-protocol` | Zod schemas for every IPC action and event. Types-only package. |
| `@ax/ipc-server` | HTTP listener (unix socket or cluster HTTP). Auth middleware. Dispatches actions to the bus. |
| `@ax/sandbox-subprocess` *(extended)* | Registers `sandbox:open-session` (new) and continues to register `sandbox:spawn` until cleanup deletes it. Spawns runner as child process, mints auth token, creates unix socket, returns handle. |
| `@ax/session-inmemory` | Registers `session:create`, `session:resolve-token`, `session:queue-work`, `session:claim-work`, `session:terminate`. In-memory for 6.5a; Week 7–9 swaps to `@ax/session-postgres` against identical contract. |
| `@ax/chat-orchestrator` | Registers `chat:run`. Calls `sandbox:open-session`, injects initial user message into inbox, receives IPC events, returns outcome. Replaces the current `chat-loop.ts`. |
| `@ax/tool-dispatcher` *(extended)* | Registers `tool:register` (called by other plugins' `init()`), owns the catalog, registers `tool:list`, routes `tool:execute:${name}` service hooks. |
| `@ax/tool-bash` *(refocused)* | Declares descriptor `{ name: 'bash', executesIn: 'sandbox', ... }` via `tool:register`. No host-side execution impl — sandbox runner has it. |
| `@ax/tool-file-io` *(refocused)* | Same pattern — declares `read_file`, `write_file` descriptors with `executesIn: 'sandbox'`. |
| `@ax/llm-anthropic` *(unchanged)* | Registers `llm:call`. |

### 6.5b (additions)

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `@ax/llm-openrouter` *(new)* | Registers `llm:call`. OpenAI-compatible wire, routed to OpenRouter endpoint. |
| `@ax/ipc-protocol` *(extended)* | Adds streaming variant of `llm.call` and `event.stream-chunk` payload schema. |
| `@ax/chat-orchestrator` / `@ax/ipc-server` *(extended)* | Fire `chat:stream-chunk` subscriber hook on receipt of `event.stream-chunk` from sandbox. |

### 6.5c (no host-side additions)

Runner change only. Host-side plugins unchanged.

### 6.5d (additions)

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `@ax/llm-proxy-anthropic-format` *(new)* | HTTP endpoint serving `POST /v1/messages` in Anthropic wire format. Translates to `llm.call` IPC action, fires the usual `llm:pre-call` / `llm:post-call` subscribers. Used by claude-sdk runner via `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL`. |

## Sandbox-side plugin decomposition

Sandbox-side code lives in the same pnpm monorepo, different build target. `@ax/ipc-protocol` is the single source of truth for the wire, imported by both host-side plugins and sandbox-side runners.

### 6.5a

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `@ax/ipc-protocol` | Shared with host-side. |
| `@ax/agent-runner-core` | IPC client (HTTP over socket/http). Long-poll loop for inbox. Local tool dispatcher (decides `executesIn: 'sandbox'` local exec vs `executesIn: 'host'` IPC forward). Delta-aware commit helper (for 6.5a: stub; Week 7–9: full). |
| `@ax/agent-native-runner` | Port of current `chat-loop.ts` behavior. Calls `llm.call` IPC in a loop, dispatches tools via runner-core's dispatcher. Entry point binary. |
| `@ax/tool-bash-impl` | Sandbox-local implementation of bash. Runs commands via `child_process.spawn`. Registered with runner-core's local dispatcher. |
| `@ax/tool-file-io-impl` | Sandbox-local read_file, write_file. |

### 6.5b (additions)

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `@ax/agent-runner-core` *(extended)* | IPC client supports streaming `llm.call`; native runner emits `event.stream-chunk` as LLM chunks arrive. |
| `@ax/agent-native-runner` *(extended)* | Wires streaming through to IPC events. |

### 6.5c (addition)

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `@ax/agent-pi-session-runner` | Embeds `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent`. Registers one custom pi-ai api (`ax-ipc`) that routes all LLM traffic through `llm.call` IPC with the configured model identifier passed verbatim. Shims `customTools` callbacks through the runner-core dispatcher. Works with every `@ax/llm-*` host plugin. |

### 6.5d (addition)

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `@ax/agent-claude-sdk-runner` | Embeds `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk`. Wires `canUseTool` + `hooks.PreToolUse` + `hooks.PostToolUse` to IPC events, registers host-mediated tools via `createSdkMcpServer`, sets `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` to the host's Anthropic-format proxy endpoint. |

Each runner is its own binary selected at sandbox-image-build or config time.

---

## Runner comparison

### `@ax/agent-native-runner` (6.5a; streaming added in 6.5b)

Simplest. A faithful port of current `chat-loop.ts` logic into the sandbox.

```
while (!done) {
  for (let msg = await ipcCall('session.next-message', {cursor}); msg.type !== 'cancel';) {
    history.push({ role: 'user', content: msg.payload })
    loop:
    while (true) {
      const response = await ipcCall('llm.call', { messages: history, tools: catalog })
      history.push(response.assistantMessage)
      fireStreamChunk(response)  // if streaming
      if (response.toolCalls.length === 0) break loop
      for (const call of response.toolCalls) {
        const pre = await ipcCall('tool.pre-call', { call })
        if (pre.verdict === 'reject') { history.push(toolErrorMsg(pre.reason)); continue }
        const output = await executeToolLocally(pre.modifiedCall ?? call) ?? await ipcCall('tool.execute-host', { call })
        ipcEvent('event.tool-post-call', { call, output })
        history.push(toolResultMsg(call, output))
      }
    }
    ipcEvent('event.turn-end', { reason: 'user-message-wait' })
    cursor = msg.cursor
  }
}
```

Non-streaming for 6.5a. Streaming lands in 6.5b alongside `chat:stream-chunk` subscriber work.

### `@ax/agent-pi-session-runner` (6.5c)

Wraps `createAgentSession()` from `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent`. Works unchanged with every `@ax/llm-*` plugin the host supports — the runner is provider-agnostic.

**LLM transport — single custom api, honest model name:**

pi-ai's `registerApiProvider` is one-api-per-call, but pi-ai itself does not pre-format requests into Anthropic/OpenAI wire shape — the registered transport receives pi-ai's normalized `Context` (messages, tools, systemPrompt) plus the `Model` object. So we register ONCE with a custom api name and handle every backing provider uniformly:

```ts
// Runner init (sandbox-side)
registerApiProvider({
  api: 'ax-ipc',
  stream: async (ctx, model, options) => {
    const response = await ipcCall('llm.call', {
      messages: normalizeMessages(ctx.messages),
      tools: ctx.tools,
      model: model.name,           // honest: configured model identifier passes through
      maxTokens: options.maxTokens,
      temperature: options.temperature,
    });
    return toPiAiResponse(response);
  },
  streamSimple: /* same */,
});

// Build the Model pi-session will see
const model = {
  api: 'ax-ipc',                   // routes to our transport
  provider: 'ax',
  name: process.env.AX_MODEL,      // e.g. 'openrouter/gpt-4.1' or 'anthropic/claude-opus-4-7'
  baseUrl: '',                     // unused by our transport
};
```

Host's `llm:call` service hook reads the `model` field from the IPC payload and routes to the configured `@ax/llm-*` plugin. OpenRouter, Anthropic, or any future provider — runner never changes.

**Do not hardcode Model.name.** v1 effectively hardcoded a Claude model name regardless of the backing call; that creates a telemetry/logging mismatch. The configured identifier must flow through honestly.

**Tools:** convert our `tool:list` catalog into pi-session's `customTools: ToolDefinition[]`. Each definition's `execute` callback does the IPC pre/post dance (per D4).

**Event translation:** subscribe to `session.subscribe()`; map pi-session events to our IPC events (`text_delta` → `event.stream-chunk`, `tool_call` is handled by our callback, `message_end` → `event.turn-end`).

**Message buffering:** pi-session uses `session.prompt(text)` once per turn, then `waitForIdle()`. Our runner loop pulls next-message from the inbox and calls `prompt`.

### `@ax/agent-claude-sdk-runner` (6.5d)

Wraps `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk`'s `query()`.

**Built-in tool policy:** keep `Bash`, `Read`, `Write`, `Edit`, `Glob`, `Grep`, `TodoWrite`. Disable `WebFetch`, `WebSearch`, `Skill`, `Task`. The kept ones run inside the sandbox process tree (claude-sdk's `sdk.mjs` → `cli.js` → tool subprocesses are all descendants of the runner, so isolation holds).

**Tool wiring:**
- `canUseTool` callback → IPC `tool.pre-call`. Works for both built-ins and MCP tools. Deny → `{behavior: 'deny', message}`; rewrite → `{behavior: 'allow', updatedInput}`.
- `hooks.PostToolUse` → IPC `event.tool-post-call` (fire-and-forget, observation-only per D4).
- `mcpServers` includes an in-process `createSdkMcpServer` registering our `executesIn: 'host'` tools (memory, web-via-host, etc., as they arrive). Each MCP handler does the IPC pre/post and returns output.

**LLM transport:** `env: { ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL: '${AX_LLM_PROXY_URL}', ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: 'ax-proxy' }`. The host runs a small Anthropic-format proxy endpoint that translates `POST /v1/messages` into the `llm.call` IPC action. This is a new host-side component introduced in 6.5b, not 6.5a.

**Event translation:** iterate the `AsyncGenerator<SDKMessage>`. `SDKAssistantMessage` with text content → `event.stream-chunk`. `SDKResultMessage` → `event.turn-end` + `event.chat-end` on final result.

---

## Tool registry

### `ToolDescriptor` shape

```ts
type ToolDescriptor = {
  name: string;              // e.g., 'bash', 'memory.recall'
  description: string;
  inputSchema: JsonSchema;   // JSON Schema Draft 2020-12
  executesIn: 'sandbox' | 'host';
}
```

### Registration flow

1. Plugin declares `calls: ['tool:register']` in its manifest.
2. In its `init()`, plugin calls `bus.call('tool:register', { ...descriptor })` for each tool it contributes.
3. Boot-time cycle detector ensures `@ax/tool-dispatcher` inits first (it registers `tool:register`).
4. For `executesIn: 'host'` tools, the same plugin also registers `tool:execute:${name}` service hook.

### Catalog delivery

At sandbox startup, runner calls `ipcCall('tool.list')` → receives the full catalog → builds its internal dispatcher.

Cache-once-per-session is fine; tool set is immutable for a session's lifetime (invariant: no runtime tool registration). If future work needs dynamic tools, the runner polls or the host pushes via the inbox.

### Why not one-plugin-per-tool

`@ax/memory-cortex` will contribute 2–3 tools (store, recall, forget) plus subscriber hooks plus service hooks (internal). Splitting each tool into its own plugin means N manifests, N packages, N test suites for one conceptual unit. The `registers: ['tool:execute:memory.store', 'tool:execute:memory.recall', 'memory:record']` manifest list is the clean path — plugins group related capabilities.

---

## Workspace coordination contract

Full implementation is Week 7–9 (`@ax/workspace-git`). The **contract** ships in 6.5a via the IPC protocol so Week 7–9 consumers land against a stable shape.

### Flow

1. Agent in sandbox modifies files via tools. Sandbox-side git is local.
2. At turn end (or per-tool-call; runner-specific), sandbox commits locally with a synthetic commit message.
3. Sandbox sends `workspace.commit-notify` RPC with `{ parentVersion, commitRef, message }`.
4. Host's `@ax/workspace-git`:
   - Fetches the commit via git-http-backend from the sandbox's git dir (subprocess mode: bind-mount; k8s mode: sidecar).
   - Builds a `WorkspaceDelta` (lazy-content-fetcher shape per ax-conventions).
   - Fires `workspace:pre-apply` — subscriber chain. Any subscriber can reject.
5. If rejected: host returns `{ accepted: false, reason }`. Sandbox `git reset --hard parentVersion`; surfaces an error to the model on next turn.
6. If accepted: host promotes the commit to its authoritative store, fires `workspace:applied`, returns `{ accepted: true, version, delta }`.

### Subscribers that will hang off this contract (future slices)

- **Skill validator** (`workspace:pre-apply`) — rejects malformed skill files.
- **Canary** (`workspace:pre-apply`) — rejects commits containing secrets.
- **Skill indexer** (`workspace:applied`) — updates search index.
- **Audit** (`workspace:applied`) — logs the commit.

### Rewind semantics

The sandbox must be able to undo a rejected commit cleanly. `@ax/agent-runner-core` provides a `commitAndNotify(parentVersion, message)` helper that:
1. Does `git commit`.
2. Calls `workspace.commit-notify`.
3. On rejection: `git reset --hard parentVersion`; surfaces `WorkspaceRejectedError` to the runner's tool-loop.
4. On accept: returns the new `version` token.

Runners surface the rejection to the model as a tool error so the model can retry with corrections.

### 6.5a scope

No `@ax/workspace-git` ships. No subscribers wired. The `workspace.commit-notify` action exists in `@ax/ipc-protocol` schemas; the host's `@ax/ipc-server` returns `{ accepted: true, version: '<stub>', delta: null }` unconditionally. Agent's file edits work; no host-side diff awareness.

6.5a acceptance test does not cover workspace. Week 7–9 is when the contract earns its keep.

---

## Boundary review — the five invariants

### I1. Hook surface is transport- and storage-agnostic

- `ToolDescriptor.executesIn: 'sandbox' | 'host'` — closed set, no backend-specific values. ✅
- `WorkspaceVersion` — opaque token, no git vocabulary in payloads. ✅
- `session.next-message` — no postgres-specific fields; `cursor` is just a monotonic int. ✅
- `IPC transport` — abstracted behind `@ax/ipc-protocol`; unix socket vs cluster HTTP is a per-plugin implementation detail. ✅

### I2. No cross-plugin imports

All cross-plugin communication via hook bus (host-side) and IPC protocol (host↔sandbox). Only `@ax/core`, `@ax/ipc-protocol`, and `@ax/agent-runner-core` are shared imports.

The runner imports `@ax/agent-runner-core`; that's one-way. Runner does not import `@ax/tool-bash` (host-side plugin) — tool descriptors arrive over IPC; sandbox-local implementations live in separate `-impl` packages. ✅

### I3. No half-wired plugins

Every new 6.5a plugin is fully wired end-to-end in its shipping PR:

- `@ax/ipc-server` → called by `@ax/chat-orchestrator` → exercised in acceptance test.
- `@ax/session-inmemory` → called by `@ax/chat-orchestrator` and `@ax/ipc-server`.
- `@ax/agent-native-runner` → exercised in acceptance test.
- `@ax/llm-openrouter` → exercised in acceptance test (preset toggles to it).

No "wire this up later" packages. ✅

### I4. One source of truth per concept

- Tool catalog: `@ax/tool-dispatcher` only. Runners don't maintain their own registries.
- Session state: `@ax/session-*` only. Runner doesn't cache (reads from IPC).
- LLM credentials: host-side `@ax/llm-*` plugins. Sandbox never holds them. ✅

### I5. Capabilities explicit and minimized

- **Sandbox capabilities:** unix socket (or egress to one host URL), local filesystem scoped to workspace dir, process spawn for in-sandbox tool subprocesses. No direct DB, no cloud creds, no uncontrolled network egress.
- **IPC auth:** per-session bearer token, revocable, scoped to that session's IPC actions. Cannot impersonate another session.
- **Tool input validation:** every `tool:execute:${name}` handler validates input via Zod before execution. `tool.pre-call` subscriber chain can reject before the tool runs.
- **Untrusted content tagging:** LLM output and tool output are untrusted; `llm:pre-call` subscribers treat history content as untrusted.

**Security checklist required for every slice's PR.**
- **6.5a:** `@ax/ipc-server` is new attack surface; sandbox primitives cross the trust boundary.
- **6.5b:** `@ax/llm-openrouter` is a new third-party endpoint + new dep.
- **6.5c:** `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent` and transitive deps land inside the sandbox.
- **6.5d:** `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk` spawns a subprocess tree inside the sandbox; `@ax/llm-proxy-anthropic-format` is new host-side HTTP surface.

Three threat models apply throughout: sandbox escape, prompt injection, supply chain.

---

## Per-slice acceptance tests

### 6.5a — topology shift

End-to-end:
1. Start host process with a config that loads `@ax/sandbox-subprocess`, `@ax/session-inmemory`, `@ax/llm-anthropic`, `@ax/agent-native-runner` (via sandbox image/binary), and all tool plugins.
2. CLI sends a user message.
3. Host spawns a subprocess sandbox; runner connects over unix socket.
4. Runner polls inbox, receives user message, calls `llm.call` (routed to Anthropic).
5. LLM responds with a `bash` tool call.
6. Runner IPCs `tool.pre-call` → host fires subscribers → `allow`.
7. Runner executes bash locally in sandbox.
8. Runner IPCs `event.tool-post-call` → host fires `tool:post-call` (audit observes).
9. Runner loops back into LLM with tool result.
10. Final assistant message; runner fires `event.chat-end`; host returns outcome to CLI.

Automated test variant: mock Anthropic HTTP with a canned response chain, spawn real subprocess sandbox, assert: all IPC events fired host-side, audit plugin logged, outcome message returned. **The observable behavior of this chat must match what Week 4-6's in-process loop produced** — that's the "nothing observable changes" invariant for this slice.

### 6.5b — providers + streaming

1. Config selects `@ax/llm-openrouter` instead of `@ax/llm-anthropic`.
2. CLI sends a user message.
3. LLM responds with streaming chunks; runner emits `event.stream-chunk` per chunk.
4. CLI renders text incrementally as chunks arrive.
5. Same tool-call flow as 6.5a completes.

Automated test: mock OpenRouter HTTP with a streaming SSE response, assert `chat:stream-chunk` subscribers fire in order, final message matches.

### 6.5c — pi-session runner

1. Config sets `runner: 'pi-session'`.
2. Config sets `AX_MODEL` to any supported host provider's model (e.g., `openrouter/gpt-4.1`).
3. CLI sends a user message.
4. Sandbox starts pi-session-runner binary; pi-session runs its internal loop; LLM traffic routes through the `ax-ipc` custom api → `llm.call` IPC → host's `@ax/llm-openrouter` (or whichever is loaded).
5. Subscribers fire identically to 6.5a/6.5b (the host doesn't know pi-session is in the sandbox).

Automated test: same as 6.5a/6.5b but with pi-session runner binary. Assert: identical host-observable event sequence.

### 6.5d — claude-sdk runner

1. Config sets `runner: 'claude-sdk'`.
2. CLI sends a user message.
3. Sandbox starts claude-sdk-runner binary; SDK's `query()` runs; `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` routes LLM traffic to host's Anthropic-format proxy → `llm.call` IPC.
4. Built-in Bash tool runs inside the sandbox (subprocess tree); `canUseTool` fires `tool.pre-call` IPC; `hooks.PostToolUse` fires `event.tool-post-call` IPC.
5. MCP-registered `executesIn: 'host'` tools dispatch via `tool.execute-host` IPC.

Automated test: mock Anthropic HTTP (via the proxy), spawn real subprocess sandbox with claude-sdk-runner, assert: built-in and MCP tool calls both fire `tool:pre-call` / `tool:post-call` subscribers, outcome matches.

---

## Out of scope / deferred

| Deferred to | What |
|---|---|
| **6.5b** | `@ax/llm-openrouter`, streaming `llm.call` extensions, `chat:stream-chunk` subscriber hook |
| **6.5c** | `@ax/agent-pi-session-runner`, runner-selection config |
| **6.5d** | `@ax/agent-claude-sdk-runner`, `@ax/llm-proxy-anthropic-format` |
| **Week 7–9** | `@ax/sandbox-k8s`, `@ax/workspace-git` (and therefore `workspace:pre-apply` / `workspace:applied` subscribers), `@ax/session-postgres`, `@ax/eventbus-*`, multi-replica host coordination |
| **Week 10–12** | Canary scanner wired against `llm:pre-call`, memory-cortex tool registration, chat-ui SSE consuming `chat:stream-chunk` |
| **Week 13+** | Additional runner variants (other agent frameworks); hot-reload of tool catalog; parallel tool execution (current design is sequential) |

Not in any slice (yet):
- Docker / apple-container sandbox plugin. Subprocess is enough for 6.5a dev; k8s is Week 7–9 for prod. Docker is nice-to-have, ships when a user actually wants it.
- Dynamic tool registration mid-session. Invariant for now: catalog is session-lifetime-immutable.
- Host→sandbox push (for admin commands, etc.). Inbox handles everything we know of; revisit if a use case appears.

---

## Scope decisions for the 6.5a implementation plan

These are 6.5a-specific open questions. Each needs an explicit call before code lands. Later slices (6.5b–d) surface their own questions when their plans get written; the list below is scoped to the topology shift.

1. **Runner binary packaging.** Single binary per runner (bundled with tool impls), or runner binary + tool impls as dlopen-able modules? Recommendation: single binary per runner. Simpler, ships in one dockerfile COPY line. Re-examine if the binary gets large enough to matter.

2. **Auth token scheme.** Opaque random string (64 bytes base64)? JWT (signed, carries claims)? Recommendation: opaque random, verified against session store on every IPC call. JWT adds signing ceremony without clear benefit since we have the store anyway.

3. **IPC request timeouts.** Per-action or global default? v1 uses 5min for `agent_response`, 15min safety net, per-call configurable. Recommendation: per-action defaults in `@ax/ipc-protocol` (e.g., `llm.call` = 5min, `tool.pre-call` = 10s, `session.next-message` = 30s long-poll), global override in config.

4. **Concurrent IPC from sandbox.** Can multiple RPCs be in flight at once (e.g., streaming LLM chunks while issuing tool.pre-call for a queued call)? Recommendation: yes, HTTP supports it natively; runner-core uses an HTTP keep-alive connection pool. Ordering of fire-and-forget events is best-effort.

5. **Cursor semantics for `session.next-message`.** Monotonic int per session? Recommendation: int; inbox entries carry their own index; cursor = last-seen-index. Sandbox advances only after processing, so crashes replay from the last-processed cursor.

6. **Eventbus in 6.5a?** Not needed for single-host/single-replica, but `chat:stream-chunk` fan-out wants it once multi-replica arrives. Recommendation: skip for 6.5a, ship alongside `@ax/eventbus-postgres` in Week 7–9. Document the fan-out path in the design so nothing surprises us later.

7. **How much of `chat-loop.ts` survives.** The turn-loop shape ports to the sandbox-side runner. The host-side orchestrator is thinner — it spawns the sandbox, waits for chat:end, returns outcome. Recommendation: delete `chat-loop.ts` as part of 6.5a; don't leave the dead code around.

8. **ax.config.ts preset wiring.** `@ax/preset-local-native-anthropic`, `@ax/preset-local-native-openrouter`? Or a more flexible config-driven preset? Recommendation: keep current ax.config.ts loader; introduce named presets as sugar later when the combinatorial cost justifies them.

---

## Kickoff prompts for implementation-plan sessions

Each slice gets its own implementation plan, written in a fresh session after `/clear`. The plans share `docs/plans/2026-04-24-week-6.5-agent-sandbox-design.md` as the contract doc.

### 6.5a — topology shift

(agent sandboxing — topology shift). Read docs/plans/2026-04-24-week-6.5-agent-sandbox-design.md
first — it specifies the IPC protocol, plugin decomposition, and runner contract. Scope is
strictly the topology shift: @ax/ipc-protocol, @ax/ipc-server, @ax/sandbox-subprocess extensions
(sandbox:open-session), @ax/session-inmemory, @ax/chat-orchestrator, @ax/agent-runner-core,
@ax/agent-native-runner, tool refactor (host→sandbox). Keep @ax/llm-anthropic unchanged; no
streaming; no new providers. Acceptance: Week 4-6's chat behavior is preserved, now with the
loop running in a subprocess sandbox. Invoke security-checklist for @ax/ipc-server and the
@ax/sandbox-subprocess session-lifetime extensions. Branch off the tip of Week 4-6. The plan
should be executable via subagent-driven-development.
```

### 6.5b — providers + streaming

docs/plans/2026-04-24-week-6.5-agent-sandbox-design.md. Scope: @ax/llm-openrouter, streaming

emission, CLI incremental rendering. Acceptance: swap provider via config; CLI renders
responses incrementally. Invoke security-checklist for @ax/llm-openrouter (new third-party
endpoint). Branch off 6.5a. Executable via subagent-driven-development.

### 6.5c — pi-session runner

docs/plans/2026-04-24-week-6.5-agent-sandbox-design.md; the pi-session-runner section

Scope: @ax/agent-pi-session-runner, runner-selection config. Acceptance: swap to pi-session via
config; works with every host-side @ax/llm-* plugin without runner changes. Invoke
security-checklist for the new sandbox-side dependency tree (@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent
and its transitive deps). Branch off 6.5b. Executable via subagent-driven-development.
```

### 6.5d — claude-sdk runner

Write an implementation plan for Week 6.5d (claude-sdk runner). Read
docs/plans/2026-04-24-week-6.5-agent-sandbox-design.md; the claude-sdk-runner section

disable WebFetch/WebSearch/Skill/Task), the canUseTool/PostToolUse wiring, and the
Anthropic-format proxy requirement. Scope: @ax/agent-claude-sdk-runner,
@ax/llm-proxy-anthropic-format. Acceptance: swap to claude-sdk via config; built-ins and MCP
tools both fire tool:pre-call / tool:post-call subscribers. Invoke security-checklist for

# Project context

Confirmed facts about ax-next. Not opinions.

- `2026-04-23` — `@ax/cli` is the only package allowed to import sibling plugins; enforced by `eslint.config.mjs` exception. All other inter-plugin communication goes through the hook bus.
- `2026-04-23` — Storage hook payload contract is `{ key: string, value: Uint8Array }` — opaque bytes, no backend vocab. Any `table`/`bucket`/`rowid` field would leak.
- `2026-04-23` — pnpm 10 requires `pnpm.onlyBuiltDependencies` allowlist for packages with postinstall scripts (e.g. `better-sqlite3` prebuild-install). Without the allowlist, the build silently skips the postinstall and the package fails to load at runtime.
- `2026-04-23` — E2E tests invoke the CLI as `node dist/main.js <args>`, not `pnpm exec ax-next`. Shebang + `chmod +x` are present but tests use node directly to avoid pnpm bin-link flakiness.
- `2026-04-23` — Week 3 shipped as PR #2 (merged). Week 1–2 kernel shipped as PR #1.

6	- `2026-04-23` — When scaffolding a plugin that touches fs/net/process/untrusted input, drop a `SECURITY.md` in the package directory at scaffold time, not later. Week 3's `packages/storage-sqlite/SECURITY.md` is the template.
7	- `2026-04-23` — For ESM "am I the main module?" checks, use `import { pathToFileURL } from 'node:url'` and compare `import.meta.url === pathToFileURL(process.argv[1]).href`. `require.main === module` doesn't exist under ESM.

12	- `2026-04-23` — Don't land "we'll wire it later" infrastructure. Either a live caller reaches it in the same PR or it doesn't merge. Symmetric API pairs (`get`/`set`) with a documented near-term consumer are the narrow exception.

# Patterns

## Patterns (do these)

- `2026-04-23` — When adding a new service hook, fill in the boundary-review block in the PR description (alternate impl / leaky field names / subscriber risk / wire surface). If you can't name an alternate impl, it probably shouldn't be a hook yet.
- `2026-04-23` — When scaffolding a plugin that touches fs/net/process/untrusted input, drop a `SECURITY.md` in the package directory at scaffold time, not later. Week 3's `packages/storage-sqlite/SECURITY.md` is the template.
- `2026-04-23` — For ESM "am I the main module?" checks, use `import { pathToFileURL } from 'node:url'` and compare `import.meta.url === pathToFileURL(process.argv[1]).href`. `require.main === module` doesn't exist under ESM.

## Anti-Patterns (don't do these)

- `2026-04-23` — Don't put backend-specific field names in hook payloads (`sha`, `bucket`, `pod_name`, `table`, `rowid`). If the name only makes sense for one impl, rename before subscribers depend on it.
- `2026-04-23` — Don't land "we'll wire it later" infrastructure. Either a live caller reaches it in the same PR or it doesn't merge. Symmetric API pairs (`get`/`set`) with a documented near-term consumer are the narrow exception.

5	- `2026-04-23` — **Shebang alone isn't enough for `pnpm exec` / direct invocation.** `@ax/cli` `dist/main.js` had `#!/usr/bin/env node` but was not executable; `pnpm exec ax-next` failed. Fix: `chmod +x dist/main.js` in the postbuild script (commit 3488654). Next time: when scaffolding a CLI package, add the chmod to postbuild at scaffold time.
6	- `2026-04-23` — **`require.main === module` doesn't work in ESM.** `@ax/cli` main-module guard misfired under ESM. Fix: use `pathToFileURL(process.argv[1]).href === import.meta.url` (commit 87246cc). Next time: any ESM entry-point pattern uses `pathToFileURL`, not CJS idioms.
7	- `2026-04-23` — **kysely `^0.27.4` pulled GHSA-wmrf / GHSA-8cpq advisories.** Bumped to `^0.28.14` post-merge (commit 957288b). Next time: before pinning a new dep, run `pnpm audit` or check `npm view <pkg> --json` for advisories on the target version range, not just latest.
8

file.content:
# Mistakes

One entry per mistake. Graduates to `## Resolved` after two subsequent sessions avoid it. Never deleted.

- `2026-04-23` — **Shebang alone isn't enough for `pnpm exec` / direct invocation.** `@ax/cli` `dist/main.js` had `#!/usr/bin/env node` but was not executable; `pnpm exec ax-next` failed. Fix: `chmod +x dist/main.js` in the postbuild script (commit 3488654). Next time: when scaffolding a CLI package, add the chmod to postbuild at scaffold time.
- `2026-04-23` — **`require.main === module` doesn't work in ESM.** `@ax/cli` main-module guard misfired under ESM. Fix: use `pathToFileURL(process.argv[1]).href === import.meta.url` (commit 87246cc). Next time: any ESM entry-point pattern uses `pathToFileURL`, not CJS idioms.
- `2026-04-23` — **kysely `^0.27.4` pulled GHSA-wmrf / GHSA-8cpq advisories.** Bumped to `^0.28.14` post-merge (commit 957288b). Next time: before pinning a new dep, run `pnpm audit` or check `npm view <pkg> --json` for advisories on the target version range, not just latest.

1	# Decisions
2	
3	Architectural / process decisions. Never deleted — strikethrough if reversed.
4	
5	| Date | Decision | Rationale | Alternatives |
6	|---|---|---|---|
7	| 2026-04-23 | Ship `storage:get` / `storage:set` without `database:get-instance` abstraction | YAGNI until a second backend exists; architecture doc Section 6 defers this until `@ax/storage-postgres` lands | Introduce the abstraction now (rejected: premature, no second impl to validate shape) |
8	| 2026-04-23 | Ship `storage:get` without a runtime consumer in Week 3 | Symmetric API with `storage:set`; consumer (session lookup) lands Week 4+. Documented waiver in PR #2 | Wait until Week 4 to register `storage:get` (rejected: half-wired feels worse than a one-slice-early symmetric pair) |
9	| 2026-04-23 | `@ax/audit-log` owns the `chat:<reqId>` key namespace in storage | One-source-of-truth invariant — any other plugin wanting to read audit records does so through a future service hook, not by reading the key directly | Shared key convention across plugins (rejected: violates invariant #4) |
10

file.content:
# Decisions

Architectural / process decisions. Never deleted — strikethrough if reversed.

| Date | Decision | Rationale | Alternatives |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2026-04-23 | Ship `storage:get` / `storage:set` without `database:get-instance` abstraction | YAGNI until a second backend exists; architecture doc Section 6 defers this until `@ax/storage-postgres` lands | Introduce the abstraction now (rejected: premature, no second impl to validate shape) |
| 2026-04-23 | Ship `storage:get` without a runtime consumer in Week 3 | Symmetric API with `storage:set`; consumer (session lookup) lands Week 4+. Documented waiver in PR #2 | Wait until Week 4 to register `storage:get` (rejected: half-wired feels worse than a one-slice-early symmetric pair) |
| 2026-04-23 | `@ax/audit-log` owns the `chat:<reqId>` key namespace in storage | One-source-of-truth invariant — any other plugin wanting to read audit records does so through a future service hook, not by reading the key directly | Shared key convention across plugins (rejected: violates invariant #4) |

1	import { z } from 'zod';
2	
3	const EnvKey = z.string().regex(/^[A-Z_][A-Z0-9_]*$/, 'env keys must be A-Z / 0-9 / _ and start with A-Z or _');
4	
5	export const SandboxSpawnInputSchema = z.object({
6	  argv: z.array(z.string().min(1)).min(1),
7	  cwd: z.string().regex(/^\//, 'cwd must be absolute'),
8	  env: z.record(EnvKey, z.string()),
9	  stdin: z.string().optional(),
10	  timeoutMs: z.number().int().positive().max(300_000).default(30_000),
11	  maxStdoutBytes: z.number().int().positive().max(10 * 1024 * 1024).default(1_048_576),
12	  maxStderrBytes: z.number().int().positive().max(10 * 1024 * 1024).default(1_048_576),
13	});
14	
15	// Caller-side shape: defaulted fields (timeoutMs, maxStdoutBytes,
16	// maxStderrBytes) are OPTIONAL here because zod will fill them in at parse
17	// time. Consumers that hand a request to bus.call('sandbox:spawn', ...)
18	// use this type.
19	export type SandboxSpawnInput = z.input<typeof SandboxSpawnInputSchema>;
20	
21	// Post-parse shape: every defaulted field is resolved. Providers that
22	// receive a pre-parsed request (i.e. after SandboxSpawnInputSchema.parse)
23	// use this type so their field accesses don't need optional-chaining.
24	export type SandboxSpawnParsed = z.output<typeof SandboxSpawnInputSchema>;
25	
26	export const SandboxSpawnResultSchema = z.object({
27	  exitCode: z.number().int().nullable(),
28	  signal: z.string().nullable(),
29	  stdout: z.string(),
30	  stderr: z.string(),
31	  truncated: z.object({ stdout: z.boolean(), stderr: z.boolean() }),
32	  timedOut: z.boolean(),
33	});
34	
35	export type SandboxSpawnResult = z.infer<typeof SandboxSpawnResultSchema>;
36

1	import { z } from 'zod';
2	
3	/**
4	 * Schema for `ax.config.ts`. Kept `.strict()` on purpose — unknown top-level
5	 * keys are rejected, so typos get loud failures instead of silent no-ops.
6	 *
7	 * Callers (config-file authors) pass partial shapes and let defaults fill in.
8	 * Internal code works with the fully-defaulted output. Because `z.infer` only
9	 * gives the output shape (defaulted fields become required), we export both
10	 * `AxConfigInput` and `AxConfig`.
11	 */
12	export const AxConfigSchema = z
13	  .object({
14	    llm: z.enum(['anthropic', 'mock']).default('mock'),
15	    sandbox: z.enum(['subprocess']).default('subprocess'),
16	    tools: z.array(z.enum(['bash', 'file-io'])).default(['bash', 'file-io']),
17	    storage: z.enum(['sqlite']).default('sqlite'),
18	    anthropic: z
19	      .object({
20	        model: z.string().optional(),
21	        maxTokens: z.number().int().positive().optional(),
22	      })
23	      .optional(),
24	  })
25	  .strict();
26	
27	export type AxConfigInput = z.input<typeof AxConfigSchema>;
28	export type AxConfig = z.output<typeof AxConfigSchema>;
29

1	# Security — `@ax/sandbox-subprocess`
2	
3	This package registers the `sandbox:spawn` service hook. It's a trust boundary — every `tool:execute` call that wants process isolation goes through it. This note captures the walk performed under the `security-checklist` skill for the initial Week 4–6 landing.
4	
5	## Security review
6	
7	- **Sandbox:** `child_process.spawn` with `shell:false` and a fixed argv array. `argv[0]` validated against `/^[A-Za-z0-9_./-]+$/` as defense-in-depth (even though `shell:false` already blocks metachar interpretation). `env` strictly built from an allowlist (`PATH`, `HOME`, `LANG`, `LC_ALL`, `TZ`, `NODE_OPTIONS=''`); caller-supplied env is filtered to the allowlist key set BEFORE merging, and the allowlist merges LAST so parent values always win on collisions. `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` explicitly verified absent from the child's env via unit test. `cwd` is validated absolute by the Zod schema. `stdio` is `['pipe','pipe','pipe']` — no inherited IPC channel or extra file descriptors. Timeout fires `SIGKILL` (default 30s, cap 300s). `stdout` and `stderr` accumulators cap at 1 MiB each by default with a truncation flag surfaced to the caller. `child.stdin.on('error', ...)` is attached before the first write to absorb EPIPE / ECONNRESET when the child closes stdin early.
8	
9	- **Injection:** The child's `stdout` and `stderr` are returned as strings. The host layer never interpolates them into another shell, prompt, or SQL query. Content flows into the chat messages array as tool-result content — the model is the expected downstream sink, and `llm:post-call` / `tool:post-call` subscribers are the designed veto / rewrite lever for anything that shouldn't reach the model.
10	
11	- **Supply chain:** No new runtime dependencies. The package depends on `@ax/core` only; `child_process`, `fs/promises`, and `os` are Node built-ins. `zod` is re-exported through `@ax/core` and not a direct dep.
12	
13	## Known scope limits (not enforced by this plugin)
14	
15	These require OS-level primitives beyond `child_process.spawn` and are deferred to Week 7–9 (`@ax/sandbox-k8s`, where pod specs provide the primitives natively) or later hardening of this package:
16	
17	- **No uid/gid drop.** The child runs as the host user.
18	- **No `ulimit` / cgroup / namespaces.** No CPU, memory, or fd limits beyond what Node inherits from the host shell.
19	- **No network isolation.** The child inherits the host's network stack.
20	- **No filesystem namespace.** The child sees the host's filesystem (subject to the workspace-relative `cwd`).
21	
22	These limits are acceptable for the subprocess sandbox's stated purpose — preventing casual shell-injection escape and capping resource usage with timeouts and output size caps — but they are NOT sufficient for executing untrusted code. A chat where an external party controls the model's tool-call arguments should run on the k8s sandbox, not this one.
23	
24	## Boundary review
25	
26	- **Alternate impl this hook could have:** `@ax/sandbox-k8s` (Week 7–9) — spawns a pod per call. Input and output shapes identical; only the backend differs.
27	- **Payload field names that might leak:** `argv`, `cwd`, `env`, `stdin`, `timeoutMs`, `maxStdout/StderrBytes`, `exitCode`, `signal`, `truncated`, `timedOut`. All OS-process vocabulary that maps 1:1 to pod-spec equivalents. No git / sqlite / HTTP vocabulary.
28	- **Subscriber risk:** None — `sandbox:spawn` is a service hook (one producer), not a subscriber hook.
29	- **Wire surface:** NOT exposed as an IPC action this week. Tool plugins are in-process consumers only. Week 7–9 may wire it through to agent-side tool-local execution — that's a future decision.
30

# Security — `@ax/sandbox-subprocess`

This package registers the `sandbox:spawn` service hook. It's a trust boundary — every `tool:execute` call that wants process isolation goes through it. This note captures the walk performed under the `security-checklist` skill for the initial Week 4–6 landing.

## Security review

- **Sandbox:** `child_process.spawn` with `shell:false` and a fixed argv array. `argv[0]` validated against `/^[A-Za-z0-9_./-]+$/` as defense-in-depth (even though `shell:false` already blocks metachar interpretation). `env` strictly built from an allowlist (`PATH`, `HOME`, `LANG`, `LC_ALL`, `TZ`, `NODE_OPTIONS=''`); caller-supplied env is filtered to the allowlist key set BEFORE merging, and the allowlist merges LAST so parent values always win on collisions. `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` explicitly verified absent from the child's env via unit test. `cwd` is validated absolute by the Zod schema. `stdio` is `['pipe','pipe','pipe']` — no inherited IPC channel or extra file descriptors. Timeout fires `SIGKILL` (default 30s, cap 300s). `stdout` and `stderr` accumulators cap at 1 MiB each by default with a truncation flag surfaced to the caller. `child.stdin.on('error', ...)` is attached before the first write to absorb EPIPE / ECONNRESET when the child closes stdin early.

- **Injection:** The child's `stdout` and `stderr` are returned as strings. The host layer never interpolates them into another shell, prompt, or SQL query. Content flows into the chat messages array as tool-result content — the model is the expected downstream sink, and `llm:post-call` / `tool:post-call` subscribers are the designed veto / rewrite lever for anything that shouldn't reach the model.

- **Supply chain:** No new runtime dependencies. The package depends on `@ax/core` only; `child_process`, `fs/promises`, and `os` are Node built-ins. `zod` is re-exported through `@ax/core` and not a direct dep.

## Known scope limits (not enforced by this plugin)

These require OS-level primitives beyond `child_process.spawn` and are deferred to Week 7–9 (`@ax/sandbox-k8s`, where pod specs provide the primitives natively) or later hardening of this package:

- **No uid/gid drop.** The child runs as the host user.
- **No `ulimit` / cgroup / namespaces.** No CPU, memory, or fd limits beyond what Node inherits from the host shell.
- **No network isolation.** The child inherits the host's network stack.
- **No filesystem namespace.** The child sees the host's filesystem (subject to the workspace-relative `cwd`).

These limits are acceptable for the subprocess sandbox's stated purpose — preventing casual shell-injection escape and capping resource usage with timeouts and output size caps — but they are NOT sufficient for executing untrusted code. A chat where an external party controls the model's tool-call arguments should run on the k8s sandbox, not this one.

## Boundary review

- **Alternate impl this hook could have:** `@ax/sandbox-k8s` (Week 7–9) — spawns a pod per call. Input and output shapes identical; only the backend differs.
- **Payload field names that might leak:** `argv`, `cwd`, `env`, `stdin`, `timeoutMs`, `maxStdout/StderrBytes`, `exitCode`, `signal`, `truncated`, `timedOut`. All OS-process vocabulary that maps 1:1 to pod-spec equivalents. No git / sqlite / HTTP vocabulary.
- **Subscriber risk:** None — `sandbox:spawn` is a service hook (one producer), not a subscriber hook.
- **Wire surface:** NOT exposed as an IPC action this week. Tool plugins are in-process consumers only. Week 7–9 may wire it through to agent-side tool-local execution — that's a future decision.

content.content:
1	# AX v2 — Plugin Architecture Design
2	
3	**Status:** Validated brainstorm, ready to build
4	**Date:** 2026-04-22
5	**Branch:** `feat/chat-observability` (legacy reference); v2 ships in a new monorepo
6	
7	---
8	
9	## TL;DR
10	
11	AX v2 is a greenfield rewrite of AX motivated by **component robustness in isolation** — every piece should be developable, testable, and debuggable without booting the whole system. The current ~20k-LOC host monolith (where `server-completions.ts` alone is 2,400 lines) couples chat orchestration to every concern that touches it, which is why chat reliability, observability, and component boundaries have all been hard.
12	
13	The shape, in five bullets:
14	
15	- A tiny **kernel** (~1,600 LOC, ~92% smaller than today's host) owns only the things every plugin assumes exist: bootstrap, hook bus, chat loop skeleton, identity primitives, IPC primitives.
16	- A **hook bus** with two flavors: **service hooks** (exactly one plugin produces the result — DI-shaped) and **subscriber hooks** (many plugins observe / transform / veto — middleware-shaped).
17	- **Everything else is a plugin** — sandbox impls, LLM providers, memory, storage, scheduler, eventbus, channels, audit, scanners, workspace, prompt builder, multi-model routing, diagnostic collector, even chat-event logging. Plugins talk to each other only through the hook bus.
18	- **Deployment shapes fall out of plugin selection.** Docker mode and k8s mode differ only in which plugins are loaded for the same hooks. The chat loop has no idea which is running.
19	- **Monorepo, independent semver per plugin** (pnpm + changesets + tsconfig refs). Greenfield — current AX repo becomes a reference implementation we read for edge cases, not a base we mutate.
20	
21	The doc below expands each piece. Section ordering mirrors how decisions stack: boundaries first, then the bus, then the loop, then the consequences (deployment, IPC, DB, channels), then the practical concerns (testing, packaging, build order).
22	
23	---
24	
25	## Section 1 — Core boundaries
26	
27	The core ("kernel") is the smallest set of things that "every plugin assumes exists." It can't itself be plugins, because plugins assume it. Five things, ~1,600 LOC total:
28	
29	1. **Bootstrap** (~300 LOC) — load config, discover and load plugins, wire lifecycle (init / start / stop), surface boot-time errors loudly.
30	2. **Hook bus** (~200 LOC) — register / fire / dispatch hooks. Two flavors (Section 2).
31	3. **Chat completion loop** (~500 LOC) — the orchestration skeleton. Everything interesting happens via hook fires (Section 3).
32	4. **Identity / context primitives** (~200 LOC) — `reqId`, `sessionId`, structured logger (already bound with `reqId`), structured `PluginError` type, per-request `ChatContext`.
33	5. **IPC primitives** (~400 LOC) — length-prefixed framing, Zod schema validation, sandbox-side IPC client. NOT sandbox spawning (that's a plugin) — just the wire protocol.
34	
35	Compared to today's ~20k LOC of host code, this is roughly a 92% reduction in trusted-core surface.
36	
37	Explicitly **NOT** in core (all become plugins):
38	
39	- sandbox implementations (subprocess, docker, k8s, seatbelt)
40	- LLM providers (anthropic, openai, router, traced wrapper, mock)
41	- memory (cortex)
42	- storage (sqlite, postgres, conversations, credentials, etc.)
43	- scheduler
44	- eventbus (in-process, postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY)
45	- channels (slack, web chat UI, ink)
46	- audit
47	- credentials
48	- web proxy
49	- scanners (canary, output sanitizer)
50	- skills validation
51	- workspace (git, gcs, content-addressed stores — see Section 4.5 for the contract)
52	- prompt builder
53	- multi-model routing
54	- catalog
55	- diagnostic collector
56	- chat termination tracker
57	
58	Anything on that list that today lives in `src/host/` is a candidate for excision — not by porting the host code, but by re-implementing as a plugin against the new hook surface.
59	
60	---
61	
62	## Section 2 — Two flavors of hook
63	
64	The hook bus distinguishes two shapes. Confusing them produces incoherent designs, so they get distinct registration / dispatch primitives.
65	
66	### Service hooks — exactly one producer
67	
68	Like dependency injection. Examples:
69	
70	- `llm:call(request) → response`
71	- `sandbox:spawn(config) → SandboxHandle`
72	- `memory:recall(query) → matches`
73	- `storage:get(key) → value`
74	
75	Core picks the registered service plugin (config-driven, like AX's current static `provider-map.ts`) and calls it. **Two plugins registering for the same service is a config error caught at load time.**
76	
77	### Subscriber hooks — many observers / transformers
78	
79	Called in order. Each subscriber sees the payload and can return a modified payload, pass it through, or `reject({ reason })`. Examples:
80	
81	- `chat:start(ctx)` — observers (audit log, metrics)
82	- `tool:pre-call(name, input) → modified-input | reject` — middleware (security scanner can veto a `bash` call; canary injector can rewrite input)
83	- `tool:post-call(name, input, output) → modified-output` — middleware (output scanner, taint tagger)
84	- `llm:pre-call(request) → modified-request` — middleware (history compactor, system prompt injector)
85	- `llm:post-call(request, response) → modified-response` — middleware (response sanitizer)
86	- `chat:end(ctx, outcome)` — observers (chat_complete logger, cost meter)
87	- `workspace:pre-apply(ctx, { changes, parent, reason }) → modified | reject` — middleware (secret scanner vetoes secrets before they reach storage)
88	- `workspace:applied(ctx, delta)` — observers (skill validator runs, indexer updates, audit log) — see Section 4.5
89	- `sandbox:exit(sandboxId, reason)` — observers (cleanup, lifecycle audit)
90	
91	**Rejection short-circuits with a structured failure that core surfaces as `chat_terminated`.** Observers that throw are isolated (caught + logged at error level + chat continues — never take down the host).
92	
93	### Why two flavors
94	
95	The natural shape differs. Service = "I'm THE one who does this." Subscriber = "I want to react / intercept." Trying to unify them produces awkward APIs (e.g., having to pick one subscriber's return value as canonical). Distinct primitives keep both shapes clean.
96	
97	---
98	
99	## Section 3 — The core chat loop
100	
101	The orchestration skeleton in core. It's meant to be read end-to-end:
102	
103	```typescript
104	async function runChat(ctx: ChatContext, message: Message): Promise<ChatOutcome> {
105	  await hooks.fire('chat:start', ctx, message);
106	  try {
107	    let messages = [message];
108	    while (true) {
109	      const llmRequest = await hooks.fire('llm:pre-call', ctx, { messages, tools: ctx.tools });
110	      let response = await hooks.call('llm:call', ctx, llmRequest);
111	      response = await hooks.fire('llm:post-call', ctx, llmRequest, response);
112	
113	      messages.push(response.assistantMessage);
114	      if (response.toolCalls.length === 0) break;
115	
116	      for (const toolCall of response.toolCalls) {
117	        const input = await hooks.fire('tool:pre-call', ctx, toolCall);
118	        if (input.rejected) { messages.push(rejectionMessage(input)); continue; }
119	        let output = await hooks.call('tool:execute', ctx, input);
120	        output = await hooks.fire('tool:post-call', ctx, input, output);
121	        messages.push(toolResult(toolCall, output));
122	      }
123	    }
124	    const outcome: ChatOutcome = { kind: 'complete', messages };
125	    await hooks.fire('chat:end', ctx, outcome);
126	    return outcome;
127	  } catch (err) {
128	    const outcome: ChatOutcome = { kind: 'terminated', reason: classify(err), error: err };
129	    await hooks.fire('chat:end', ctx, outcome);
130	    return outcome;
131	  }
132	}
133	```
134	
135	Roughly 50 lines. Compare to today's 2,400-line `server-completions.ts`.
136	
137	What's no longer in this loop (now plugins):
138	
139	- **Skills validation** — subscribed to `workspace:applied`, runs out-of-band.
140	- **Memory recall** — `llm:pre-call` subscriber injects memory into messages.
141	- **History compaction** — `llm:pre-call` subscriber.
142	- **Scanner / canary injection** — `tool:pre-call` and `tool:post-call` subscribers.
143	- **Diagnostic collector** — `chat:start` creates a per-request collector; hooks push into it.
144	- **`chat_complete` / `chat_terminated` events** — `chat:end` subscriber.
145	- **Sandbox spawn** — `tool:execute` for tools that need it, OR `chat:start` subscriber if one sandbox per chat.
146	
147	### ChatContext
148	
149	Carries: `reqId`, `sessionId`, `agentId`, `userId`, `tools` (catalog), `logger` (already bound with `reqId`), and a tiny `state` map plugins can use for per-chat state. **Nothing else** — no provider references, no config. Plugins that need config get it at construction time.
150	
151	---
152	
153	## Section 4 — Deployment shapes via plugin selection
154	
155	Every difference between docker-mode and k8s-mode falls out as a different service plugin loaded for the same hook. **The core chat loop never knows which is running.**
156	
157	| Hook | docker mode | k8s mode |
158	|---|---|---|
159	| `sandbox:spawn` | sandbox-docker | sandbox-k8s |
160	| `ipc:transport` | ipc-unix-socket | ipc-http |
161	| `workspace:provision` | workspace-local-git | workspace-git-http (talks to ax-git pod) |
162	| `storage:get` / `set` | storage-sqlite | storage-postgres |
163	| `eventbus:emit` / `subscribe` | eventbus-inprocess | eventbus-postgres (LISTEN/NOTIFY) |
164	| `session:lookup` / `store` | session-inmemory | session-postgres |
165	| `audit:write` | audit-sqlite | audit-postgres |
166	
167	A "profile" is just a set of plugins. Today's `config.providers` already does this for some categories; the new design pushes it all the way down. `ax-values.yaml` becomes essentially: "load these plugins, configure them like so." No code change to switch deployment shapes.
168	
169	The multi-replica concern is fully absorbed because every "where does this state live" question routes through a service plugin: N replicas can run concurrently because they all read / write through `storage-postgres` and coordinate via `eventbus-postgres`. Core has no in-memory cross-request state. Session affinity (which replica owns SSE stream X?) is the session plugin's responsibility.
170	
171	### CRITICAL CONSTRAINT
172	
173	**The hook surface MUST be transport-agnostic.** The moment a hook payload includes a Unix socket path or HTTP URL, the abstraction has leaked. `sandbox:spawn(config) → SandboxHandle` — `SandboxHandle` exposes `send(msg)` / `recv()` / `kill()`. The k8s impl wraps HTTP; the docker impl wraps a Unix socket. Core sees neither.
174	
175	### Plugin-to-plugin dependencies
176	
177	E.g., k8s sandbox writes pod metadata to storage. Plugins call other service hooks directly — `storage.set('sandbox-pods', ...)`. **No DI container.** Core only guarantees: every plugin's `init()` runs after all service hooks are registered.
178	
179	### The git-server case
180	
181	Stays as a separate container; the `workspace-git` plugin (in HTTP mode) just talks to it. The "plugin" is the client talking to it, not the server itself. Out-of-process services not on the hot path don't need to be plugins; they're just dependencies the right plugin client knows how to reach.
182	
183	---
184	
185	## Section 4.5 — Workspace abstraction
186	
187	The workspace contract has to outlive its first backend. Today we'd build it for git; tomorrow we'd want GCS, S3, or a content-addressed object store. The hook surface MUST not leak any of git's vocabulary (sha, branch, bundle, ref, commit) — same rule as Section 4's transport-agnostic constraint, applied to storage.
188	
189	This matters for security: skill validation, secret scanning, and audit are all delta-driven. If the delta shape leaks git semantics, every scanner becomes a coupled rewrite when a non-git backend shows up. Get the surface right at Week 1; ship `@ax/workspace-gcs` as a Week-13 additive plugin with no subscriber changing.
190	
191	### The primitive: opaque versions over snapshots
192	
193	`WorkspaceVersion` is an opaque token. Subscribers never parse it; they pass it back to workspace hooks. Git impl makes it a commit SHA; GCS impl makes it a manifest object name. Neither leaks.
194	
195	A "workspace state" is a snapshot — the full set of `path → content` at a point in time. Deltas between snapshots are derived. This is the GCS-natural shape; git can always derive it (`git ls-tree` + `git diff-tree`). The reverse — building snapshots out of commits in a GCS impl — would require GCS to invent commit semantics it doesn't have.

# AX v2 — Plugin Architecture Design

**Status:** Validated brainstorm, ready to build
**Date:** 2026-04-22
**Branch:** `feat/chat-observability` (legacy reference); v2 ships in a new monorepo

---

## TL;DR

AX v2 is a greenfield rewrite of AX motivated by **component robustness in isolation** — every piece should be developable, testable, and debuggable without booting the whole system. The current ~20k-LOC host monolith (where `server-completions.ts` alone is 2,400 lines) couples chat orchestration to every concern that touches it, which is why chat reliability, observability, and component boundaries have all been hard.

The shape, in five bullets:

- A tiny **kernel** (~1,600 LOC, ~92% smaller than today's host) owns only the things every plugin assumes exist: bootstrap, hook bus, chat loop skeleton, identity primitives, IPC primitives.
- A **hook bus** with two flavors: **service hooks** (exactly one plugin produces the result — DI-shaped) and **subscriber hooks** (many plugins observe / transform / veto — middleware-shaped).
- **Everything else is a plugin** — sandbox impls, LLM providers, memory, storage, scheduler, eventbus, channels, audit, scanners, workspace, prompt builder, multi-model routing, diagnostic collector, even chat-event logging. Plugins talk to each other only through the hook bus.
- **Deployment shapes fall out of plugin selection.** Docker mode and k8s mode differ only in which plugins are loaded for the same hooks. The chat loop has no idea which is running.
- **Monorepo, independent semver per plugin** (pnpm + changesets + tsconfig refs). Greenfield — current AX repo becomes a reference implementation we read for edge cases, not a base we mutate.

The doc below expands each piece. Section ordering mirrors how decisions stack: boundaries first, then the bus, then the loop, then the consequences (deployment, IPC, DB, channels), then the practical concerns (testing, packaging, build order).

---

## Section 1 — Core boundaries

The core ("kernel") is the smallest set of things that "every plugin assumes exists." It can't itself be plugins, because plugins assume it. Five things, ~1,600 LOC total:

1. **Bootstrap** (~300 LOC) — load config, discover and load plugins, wire lifecycle (init / start / stop), surface boot-time errors loudly.
2. **Hook bus** (~200 LOC) — register / fire / dispatch hooks. Two flavors (Section 2).
3. **Chat completion loop** (~500 LOC) — the orchestration skeleton. Everything interesting happens via hook fires (Section 3).
4. **Identity / context primitives** (~200 LOC) — `reqId`, `sessionId`, structured logger (already bound with `reqId`), structured `PluginError` type, per-request `ChatContext`.
5. **IPC primitives** (~400 LOC) — length-prefixed framing, Zod schema validation, sandbox-side IPC client. NOT sandbox spawning (that's a plugin) — just the wire protocol.

Compared to today's ~20k LOC of host code, this is roughly a 92% reduction in trusted-core surface.

Explicitly **NOT** in core (all become plugins):

- sandbox implementations (subprocess, docker, k8s, seatbelt)
- LLM providers (anthropic, openai, router, traced wrapper, mock)
- memory (cortex)
- storage (sqlite, postgres, conversations, credentials, etc.)
- scheduler
- eventbus (in-process, postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY)
- channels (slack, web chat UI, ink)
- audit
- credentials
- web proxy
- scanners (canary, output sanitizer)
- skills validation
- workspace (git, gcs, content-addressed stores — see Section 4.5 for the contract)
- prompt builder
- multi-model routing
- catalog
- diagnostic collector
- chat termination tracker

Anything on that list that today lives in `src/host/` is a candidate for excision — not by porting the host code, but by re-implementing as a plugin against the new hook surface.

---

## Section 2 — Two flavors of hook

The hook bus distinguishes two shapes. Confusing them produces incoherent designs, so they get distinct registration / dispatch primitives.

### Service hooks — exactly one producer

Like dependency injection. Examples:

- `llm:call(request) → response`
- `sandbox:spawn(config) → SandboxHandle`
- `memory:recall(query) → matches`
- `storage:get(key) → value`

Core picks the registered service plugin (config-driven, like AX's current static `provider-map.ts`) and calls it. **Two plugins registering for the same service is a config error caught at load time.**

### Subscriber hooks — many observers / transformers

Called in order. Each subscriber sees the payload and can return a modified payload, pass it through, or `reject({ reason })`. Examples:

- `chat:start(ctx)` — observers (audit log, metrics)
- `tool:pre-call(name, input) → modified-input | reject` — middleware (security scanner can veto a `bash` call; canary injector can rewrite input)
- `tool:post-call(name, input, output) → modified-output` — middleware (output scanner, taint tagger)
- `llm:pre-call(request) → modified-request` — middleware (history compactor, system prompt injector)
- `llm:post-call(request, response) → modified-response` — middleware (response sanitizer)
- `chat:end(ctx, outcome)` — observers (chat_complete logger, cost meter)
- `workspace:pre-apply(ctx, { changes, parent, reason }) → modified | reject` — middleware (secret scanner vetoes secrets before they reach storage)
- `workspace:applied(ctx, delta)` — observers (skill validator runs, indexer updates, audit log) — see Section 4.5
- `sandbox:exit(sandboxId, reason)` — observers (cleanup, lifecycle audit)

**Rejection short-circuits with a structured failure that core surfaces as `chat_terminated`.** Observers that throw are isolated (caught + logged at error level + chat continues — never take down the host).

### Why two flavors

The natural shape differs. Service = "I'm THE one who does this." Subscriber = "I want to react / intercept." Trying to unify them produces awkward APIs (e.g., having to pick one subscriber's return value as canonical). Distinct primitives keep both shapes clean.

---

## Section 3 — The core chat loop

The orchestration skeleton in core. It's meant to be read end-to-end:

```typescript
async function runChat(ctx: ChatContext, message: Message): Promise<ChatOutcome> {
  await hooks.fire('chat:start', ctx, message);
  try {
    let messages = [message];
    while (true) {
      const llmRequest = await hooks.fire('llm:pre-call', ctx, { messages, tools: ctx.tools });
      let response = await hooks.call('llm:call', ctx, llmRequest);
      response = await hooks.fire('llm:post-call', ctx, llmRequest, response);

      messages.push(response.assistantMessage);
      if (response.toolCalls.length === 0) break;

      for (const toolCall of response.toolCalls) {
        const input = await hooks.fire('tool:pre-call', ctx, toolCall);
        if (input.rejected) { messages.push(rejectionMessage(input)); continue; }
        let output = await hooks.call('tool:execute', ctx, input);
        output = await hooks.fire('tool:post-call', ctx, input, output);
        messages.push(toolResult(toolCall, output));
      }
    }
    const outcome: ChatOutcome = { kind: 'complete', messages };
    await hooks.fire('chat:end', ctx, outcome);
    return outcome;
  } catch (err) {
    const outcome: ChatOutcome = { kind: 'terminated', reason: classify(err), error: err };
    await hooks.fire('chat:end', ctx, outcome);
    return outcome;
  }
}
```

Roughly 50 lines. Compare to today's 2,400-line `server-completions.ts`.

What's no longer in this loop (now plugins):

- **Skills validation** — subscribed to `workspace:applied`, runs out-of-band.
- **Memory recall** — `llm:pre-call` subscriber injects memory into messages.
- **History compaction** — `llm:pre-call` subscriber.
- **Scanner / canary injection** — `tool:pre-call` and `tool:post-call` subscribers.
- **Diagnostic collector** — `chat:start` creates a per-request collector; hooks push into it.
- **`chat_complete` / `chat_terminated` events** — `chat:end` subscriber.
- **Sandbox spawn** — `tool:execute` for tools that need it, OR `chat:start` subscriber if one sandbox per chat.

### ChatContext

Carries: `reqId`, `sessionId`, `agentId`, `userId`, `tools` (catalog), `logger` (already bound with `reqId`), and a tiny `state` map plugins can use for per-chat state. **Nothing else** — no provider references, no config. Plugins that need config get it at construction time.

---

## Section 4 — Deployment shapes via plugin selection

Every difference between docker-mode and k8s-mode falls out as a different service plugin loaded for the same hook. **The core chat loop never knows which is running.**

| Hook | docker mode | k8s mode |
|---|---|---|
| `sandbox:spawn` | sandbox-docker | sandbox-k8s |
| `ipc:transport` | ipc-unix-socket | ipc-http |
| `workspace:provision` | workspace-local-git | workspace-git-http (talks to ax-git pod) |
| `storage:get` / `set` | storage-sqlite | storage-postgres |
| `eventbus:emit` / `subscribe` | eventbus-inprocess | eventbus-postgres (LISTEN/NOTIFY) |
| `session:lookup` / `store` | session-inmemory | session-postgres |
| `audit:write` | audit-sqlite | audit-postgres |

A "profile" is just a set of plugins. Today's `config.providers` already does this for some categories; the new design pushes it all the way down. `ax-values.yaml` becomes essentially: "load these plugins, configure them like so." No code change to switch deployment shapes.

The multi-replica concern is fully absorbed because every "where does this state live" question routes through a service plugin: N replicas can run concurrently because they all read / write through `storage-postgres` and coordinate via `eventbus-postgres`. Core has no in-memory cross-request state. Session affinity (which replica owns SSE stream X?) is the session plugin's responsibility.

### CRITICAL CONSTRAINT

**The hook surface MUST be transport-agnostic.** The moment a hook payload includes a Unix socket path or HTTP URL, the abstraction has leaked. `sandbox:spawn(config) → SandboxHandle` — `SandboxHandle` exposes `send(msg)` / `recv()` / `kill()`. The k8s impl wraps HTTP; the docker impl wraps a Unix socket. Core sees neither.

### Plugin-to-plugin dependencies

E.g., k8s sandbox writes pod metadata to storage. Plugins call other service hooks directly — `storage.set('sandbox-pods', ...)`. **No DI container.** Core only guarantees: every plugin's `init()` runs after all service hooks are registered.

### The git-server case

Stays as a separate container; the `workspace-git` plugin (in HTTP mode) just talks to it. The "plugin" is the client talking to it, not the server itself. Out-of-process services not on the hot path don't need to be plugins; they're just dependencies the right plugin client knows how to reach.

---

## Section 4.5 — Workspace abstraction

The workspace contract has to outlive its first backend. Today we'd build it for git; tomorrow we'd want GCS, S3, or a content-addressed object store. The hook surface MUST not leak any of git's vocabulary (sha, branch, bundle, ref, commit) — same rule as Section 4's transport-agnostic constraint, applied to storage.

This matters for security: skill validation, secret scanning, and audit are all delta-driven. If the delta shape leaks git semantics, every scanner becomes a coupled rewrite when a non-git backend shows up. Get the surface right at Week 1; ship `@ax/workspace-gcs` as a Week-13 additive plugin with no subscriber changing.

### The primitive: opaque versions over snapshots

`WorkspaceVersion` is an opaque token. Subscribers never parse it; they pass it back to workspace hooks. Git impl makes it a commit SHA; GCS impl makes it a manifest object name. Neither leaks.

A "workspace state" is a snapshot — the full set of `path → content` at a point in time. Deltas between snapshots are derived. This is the GCS-natural shape; git can always derive it (`git ls-tree` + `git diff-tree`). The reverse — building snapshots out of commits in a GCS impl — would require GCS to invent commit semantics it doesn't have.

### Service hooks (workspace plugin registers exactly one impl)

```typescript
type WorkspaceVersion = string & { __brand: 'WorkspaceVersion' };
